title: Kinetic transport in quasicrystals
speaker: Jens Marklof
abstract: Previous studies of kinetic transport in the Lorentz gas have been limited to cases where the scatterers are distributed at random (e.g. at the points of a spatial Poisson process) or at the vertices of a Euclidean lattice. In this talk I will report on recent joint work with Andreas Strombergsson (Uppsala) on quasicrystalline scatterer configurations, which are non-periodic, yet strongly correlated. A famous example is the vertex set of the Penrose tiling.

proof_stages.label: Boltzmann-Grad Lorentz-gas setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the classical low-density Lorentz gas, rescales time and position by the mean free path of order rho^{-(d-1)}, and recalls that for Poisson scatterers the limiting density satisfies the linear Boltzmann equation because successive collisions become independent.

proof_stages.label: Periodic kinetic limit with hidden memory
proof_stages.summary: For periodic scatterers the rescaled particle densities still converge, but the limiting operators on ordinary phase space fail to form a semigroup, and Markovianity is recovered only after adjoining the time to the next hit and the outgoing velocity after that hit.

proof_stages.label: Random-lattice proof of periodic free paths
proof_stages.summary: The periodic first-collision problem is reduced to asking whether a rescaled random lattice or affine lattice avoids a fixed cylinder, and equidistribution in the space of lattices gives the limiting free-path distribution and its polynomial tail behavior.

proof_stages.label: Cut-and-project quasicrystal scatterers
proof_stages.summary: Quasicrystal scatterer sets are introduced via the cut-and-project construction from a higher-dimensional lattice and a window in internal space, producing Delone point sets with well-defined spatial density.

proof_stages.label: Ratner hull and space of quasicrystals
proof_stages.summary: The SL_d deformations used in the free-path scaling are lifted to the ambient homogeneous space, and Ratner theory supplies a closed subgroup H_g with finite Haar measure whose orbit closure becomes the probability space of quasicrystal configurations.

proof_stages.label: Quasicrystal free-path limit formula
proof_stages.summary: The limiting probability of a long free path in the original quasicrystal is expressed as the Haar probability that a random quasicrystal in the Ratner hull has no point in the fixed cylinder, with concrete H_g identifications in generic and algebraic cases.

proof_stages.label: Extensions and open point-process program
proof_stages.summary: The same homogeneous-dynamics framework treats unions of incommensurable lattices, giving product free-path laws and arbitrary polynomial tail exponents, and the talk ends by asking for natural invariant point processes not derived from lattices or Poisson constructions.

examples.engagement_reason: For Poisson scatterers he writes the limiting transition kernel explicitly, using the exponential free-path law and differential cross section to recover the linear Boltzmann equation.

examples.name: Penrose tiling
examples.note: The Penrose tiling is used as the familiar cut-and-project quasicrystal and later as the concrete algebraic case whose Ratner subgroup is related to a Hilbert modular group.
examples.engagement_reason: For the Penrose tiling he traces its cut-and-project origin and identifies the associated Ratner subgroup as an embedded SL(2,R)^2 with a Hilbert modular lattice.

examples.name: Superposition of incommensurable lattices
examples.note: Superimposing rationally unrelated lattices produces the product limit law, visibly yielding the idea that one can realize arbitrary polynomial free-path tail exponents.
examples.engagement_reason: For incommensurable lattice superpositions he applies the machinery to show the limiting free-path distribution factors as a product and derives arbitrary polynomial decay powers.
